article_meta.title: A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom weari

reasoning: The model has two issues: (1) The first flagged item ('an extraordinary misstep') is a quote from Jacob Ward, a technology journalist, attributed to him in the article. This is an attribution rule violation. (2) The second flagged item ('claims that strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth') is genuinely the author's own prose and is a defensible bias flag, though the bias type labels used ('Loaded Language' and 'Framing') are not from the approved list. The model also misses some potential author-level bias like 'high-stakes trial' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective), 'closely watched trial' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective), and the overall framing/slant of the article.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: The first flagged item ('an extraordinary misstep' by Meta) is clearly attributed to Jacob Ward in the article text ('Ward described the episode as...'). This is an attribution rule violation. The second item is the author's own prose, so that's correct.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: One of two items is an attribution rule violation (the Ward quote). The second item about 'strike at the heart of the national debate' is a defensible catch of author editorializing. So roughly half the output is a false positive.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed several instances: 'high-stakes trial' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective), 'closely watched trial' (Subjective Qualifying Adjective), 'scolded' in the headline/lede (Word Choice - loaded verb). These are moderate misses.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: 'Loaded Language' and 'Framing' are not on the approved bias type list. The correct labels would be something like 'Sensationalism/Emotionalism' or 'Spin' for the second item. Both labels are outside the defined taxonomy.
